metadata.title: Instrument Cluster — Safety Mechanisms System Requirements Specification (Infineon TRAVEO T2G MCU)
metadata.author: Systems Engineering Team
metadata.notes: Safety mechanisms reference Infineon TRAVEO T2G Body Controller Safety Manual (CYT4BF/CYT4BB device family). All coverage claims and diagnostic coverage percentages shall be validated against the applicable device safety manual revision before safety case submission.

requirements.title: CPU dual-core lockstep (DCLS) operation
requirements.text: The instrument cluster safety software SHALL configure and maintain the TRAVEO T2G main CPU cluster in Dual-Core Lockstep (DCLS) mode, where the primary CM7_0 core and the lockstep CM7_1 core execute identical instruction streams in lock-step with a defined pipeline delay. The hardware Compare Logic SHALL continuously compare outputs of both cores on every clock cycle. Any mismatch detected by the Compare Logic SHALL be reported immediately to the FAULT Collection and Control Unit (FAULT unit) as a CPU lockstep fault and SHALL trigger the fault response action configured in SYS-IC-SAF-008. DCLS mode SHALL be enabled during startup initialization and SHALL NOT be disabled during normal vehicle operation.
requirements.rationale: Single-point CPU core faults (bit-flip in instruction decode, ALU error, register corruption) are the highest-probability hardware failure mode in the application processor. TRAVEO T2G DCLS provides hardware-enforced dual-channel CPU redundancy per ISO 26262 Part 5 safety mechanism class HW-SM-CPU-01. DCLS achieves a diagnostic coverage of >= 99% for CPU random hardware failures per the TRAVEO T2G safety manual, enabling ASIL-B SPFM and LFM target achievement without external redundancy. Reference: Infineon TRAVEO T2G Safety Manual, Section 4.2 (CPU Lockstep Configuration).

requirements.title: Startup BIST execution before normal operation
requirements.text: The instrument cluster safety software SHALL execute the full TRAVEO T2G startup Built-In Self-Test (BIST) sequence during the power-on initialization phase before transitioning to normal operating mode. The startup BIST SHALL include: (1) CPU BIST — testing the CM7 core register file, ALU, and pipeline logic using the device-provided CPU BIST routines; (2) Memory BIST (MBIST) — testing all SRAM banks designated as safety-relevant in the TRAVEO T2G safety manual using the MBIST controller; (3) Flash ECC verification — verifying the ECC integrity of the application code and calibration data partitions. The total startup BIST duration SHALL not exceed 200 ms. Any BIST test that reports a failure SHALL prevent the system from transitioning to normal operating mode, SHALL set the cluster display to the startup fault state (blank display with a solid fault indicator on KL30), and SHALL store a BIST failure DTC in NVM.
requirements.rationale: Startup BIST detects latent hardware faults accumulated during the previous operating cycle (storage, temperature cycling, radiation) before the system enters safety-relevant operation. Without startup BIST, a latent CPU or SRAM fault from a prior cycle would remain undetected throughout the current drive cycle, violating the LFM target. 200 ms budget is derived from the power-on display-ready requirement SYS-IC-PWR-005 (2000 ms total) minus application boot time allocation. Reference: Infineon TRAVEO T2G Safety Manual, Section 5.1 (Startup Self-Test Sequence).

requirements.title: Flash ECC single-bit correction and double-bit detection
requirements.text: The instrument cluster safety software SHALL enable the TRAVEO T2G flash controller ECC (Error Correction Code) for all flash partitions containing safety-relevant application code and calibration data. The flash ECC SHALL implement single-bit error correction (SECDED scheme): single-bit errors SHALL be corrected transparently by hardware and SHALL generate a correctable-ECC fault notification to the FAULT unit; double-bit (uncorrectable) errors SHALL generate an uncorrectable-ECC fault notification to the FAULT unit and SHALL trigger the immediate safe state transition defined in SYS-IC-SAF-012. The safety software SHALL maintain a running count of single-bit ECC corrections per flash page; if any page accumulates more than three single-bit corrections within one drive cycle, it SHALL be treated as a degraded flash region and a maintenance DTC SHALL be set.
requirements.rationale: Flash bit-flips due to charge loss, radiation, or write/erase wear are the dominant flash reliability failure mode. Uncorrected flash errors in the application code partition would cause incorrect instruction execution, which may not be caught by CPU lockstep alone if both cores fetch the same corrupted instruction. Flash ECC provides an independent detection layer for this failure mode. Single-bit correction accumulation monitoring detects progressive flash degradation before it advances to uncorrectable errors. Reference: Infineon TRAVEO T2G Safety Manual, Section 6.1 (Flash ECC Configuration).

requirements.title: SRAM ECC monitoring and periodic scrubbing
requirements.text: The instrument cluster safety software SHALL enable the TRAVEO T2G SRAM ECC for all SRAM banks containing safety-relevant runtime data (stack, safety variable storage, E2E buffers, fault status). SRAM single-bit errors SHALL be corrected transparently and reported to the FAULT unit; SRAM double-bit errors SHALL trigger the safe state transition defined in SYS-IC-SAF-012. The safety software SHALL implement a periodic SRAM scrubbing routine that reads and rewrites every SRAM location used by safety-relevant data at a minimum interval of 10 ms, ensuring that accumulation of multi-bit errors in infrequently accessed memory regions is detected and corrected before becoming uncorrectable. The scrubbing interval SHALL be validated against the device FTTI defined in HARA HA-IC-SAF-002.
requirements.rationale: SRAM is vulnerable to both single-event upsets (radiation-induced bit-flips) and systematic cell degradation. A single-bit SRAM error in a safety-relevant variable (e.g., ASIL signal validity flag, fault status register shadow copy) can corrupt safety decisions without being detected by CPU lockstep, since both lockstep cores read the same corrupted SRAM value. Periodic scrubbing prevents multi-bit accumulation in locations that are read infrequently. Reference: Infineon TRAVEO T2G Safety Manual, Section 6.2 (SRAM ECC and Scrubbing).

requirements.title: Windowed Watchdog Timer (WWDT) configuration and servicing
requirements.text: The instrument cluster safety software SHALL configure the TRAVEO T2G Multi-Counter Watchdog Timer (MCWDT) in windowed watchdog mode for the main application task. The watchdog service window SHALL be defined with a lower bound of 50% and an upper bound of 100% of the configured watchdog period (nominal period: 10 ms). The application safety task SHALL service the watchdog by writing the correct service sequence only within the permitted service window. A service write outside the window (too early: before 50%, too late: after 100%) SHALL trigger an immediate WWDT reset of the device. The watchdog period and window bounds SHALL be locked via the TRAVEO T2G register lock mechanism after initialization to prevent runtime modification. A separate MCWDT counter instance SHALL be reserved for the CM0+ supervisory CPU and SHALL not share the service sequence of the main application watchdog.
requirements.rationale: The windowed watchdog detects both program flow failures (task overrun or deadlock, caught by late servicing) and runaway execution anomalies (pathological loop that services the watchdog too frequently, caught by early servicing). A conventional non-windowed watchdog cannot detect runaway execution. The 10 ms period is derived from the application scheduler cycle; the 50% lower bound prevents service from a task that has lost its timing reference. Reference: Infineon TRAVEO T2G Safety Manual, Section 7.1 (MCWDT Windowed Watchdog Configuration).

requirements.title: Clock Monitoring (CMC) for safety-relevant clock sources
requirements.text: The instrument cluster safety software SHALL configure the TRAVEO T2G Clock Monitor Circuit (CMC) to monitor all safety-relevant clock sources, including the primary high-frequency oscillator (ECO or IMO used as system clock source) and the CM7 core clock. The CMC SHALL use the device Watch Crystal Oscillator (WCO, 32.768 kHz) as the independent reference clock for frequency monitoring. The CMC SHALL be configured to generate a FAULT unit notification if the monitored clock frequency deviates by more than 10% from its nominal value or if a complete clock loss is detected. On CMC fault, the fault response action defined in SYS-IC-SAF-008 SHALL be executed within one reference clock period. The WCO SHALL be validated at startup BIST before being used as the CMC reference.
requirements.rationale: An undetected CPU clock frequency deviation causes all timing-critical safety functions (watchdog period, E2E timeout monitoring, FTTI-bounded response times) to operate on incorrect time bases. Clock loss is a total loss of CPU execution. The WCO is used as reference because it is an independent oscillator not shared with the system clock tree. A 10% deviation threshold is specified to catch oscillator drift, PLL lock loss, and EMC-induced clock glitches while tolerating normal crystal accuracy variation. Reference: Infineon TRAVEO T2G Safety Manual, Section 8.1 (Clock Monitor Configuration).

requirements.title: Internal voltage supply monitoring (HVSS)
requirements.text: The instrument cluster safety software SHALL enable the TRAVEO T2G High-Voltage Supply Supervisor (HVSS) and internal Brown-Out Detectors (BOD) to monitor all regulated internal supply rails, including VDDD (digital supply), VDDA (analog supply), and VCCD (core voltage). Any detected overvoltage or undervoltage exceedance on a monitored rail SHALL generate a FAULT unit notification. The safety software SHALL read and log the HVSS fault status register on every startup to detect latent supply faults from the previous power cycle. External supply monitoring requirements are defined separately in SYS-IC-PWR-001 and SYS-IC-PWR-012.
requirements.rationale: Internal regulated supply rail exceedance can cause undefined logic states, memory read errors, and oscillator frequency shifts that are not detectable by other safety mechanisms (lockstep, ECC). The TRAVEO T2G HVSS monitors internal rails independently of the external supply monitor, providing a separate diagnostic channel. ASIL-A assigned because internal supply faults are partially controlled by the external power supply function (SYS-IC-PWR-001); the residual severity is lower than a total undetected CPU fault. Reference: Infineon TRAVEO T2G Safety Manual, Section 9.1 (Voltage Supervisor Configuration).

requirements.title: FAULT Collection and Control Unit configuration and fault response
requirements.text: The instrument cluster safety software SHALL configure the TRAVEO T2G FAULT Collection and Control Unit (FAULT unit) to aggregate all safety-relevant hardware fault sources from the following subsystems: CPU Compare Logic (DCLS mismatch), Flash ECC (correctable and uncorrectable), SRAM ECC (correctable and uncorrectable), MCWDT (early and late service), CMC (frequency deviation and loss), HVSS (over/undervoltage), SMPU/PPU (access violation). Each fault source SHALL be assigned a fault response action: (a) non-recoverable faults (DCLS mismatch, uncorrectable ECC, MCWDT timeout) SHALL trigger an immediate system reset via the FAULT unit reset output; (b) recoverable faults (correctable ECC, CMC warning) SHALL trigger a software interrupt routed to the CM0+ supervisory core for safe state evaluation. All FAULT unit configuration registers SHALL be locked via the TRAVEO T2G protection context mechanism before transitioning out of the startup initialization phase.
requirements.rationale: Centralised fault aggregation in the FAULT unit ensures that every safety-relevant hardware fault has a defined, hardware-enforced response that cannot be silently suppressed by a faulty application. Locking FAULT unit configuration registers after startup prevents a software fault (or attack) from reconfiguring fault responses to mask hardware errors. The split between reset-triggering and interrupt-triggering faults reflects the distinction between immediately hazardous faults (requiring hardware reset without software intervention) and faults where software-managed safe state entry is appropriate. Reference: Infineon TRAVEO T2G Safety Manual, Section 10 (Fault Collection and Control Unit).

requirements.title: Shared Memory Protection Unit (SMPU) and Peripheral Protection Unit (PPU) configuration
requirements.text: The instrument cluster safety software SHALL configure the TRAVEO T2G Shared Memory Protection Unit (SMPU) to restrict read and write access to safety-relevant SRAM regions to the authorized CPU Protection Context (PC) only. Safety-critical data structures (fault status mirror, E2E state variables, ASIL signal buffers) SHALL be placed in SMPU-protected regions inaccessible to QM software tasks. The Peripheral Protection Unit (PPU) SHALL restrict write access to the configuration registers of all safety-relevant peripherals (FAULT unit, MCWDT, CMC, clock configuration, SMPU itself) to the startup initialization Protection Context only, preventing runtime reconfiguration. Any SMPU or PPU access violation SHALL be reported to the FAULT unit as per SYS-IC-SAF-008. SMPU and PPU configurations SHALL be finalized and locked before the QM application layer is started.
requirements.rationale: Freedom from Interference (FFI) between QM and ASIL-B software partitions is a mandatory ISO 26262 Part 6 requirement when QM and ASIL code co-execute on the same MCU. Without SMPU protection, a QM task with a runaway pointer could corrupt ASIL safety data structures. Without PPU protection, a QM software bug could reconfigure safety-critical peripheral settings (e.g., disable WWDT, clear FAULT unit mask). SMPU/PPU enforcement provides the hardware-enforced FFI barrier required to avoid ASIL decomposition of the entire software stack. Reference: Infineon TRAVEO T2G Safety Manual, Section 11 (Protection Units — SMPU and PPU).

requirements.title: On-chip die temperature monitoring
requirements.text: The instrument cluster safety software SHALL read the TRAVEO T2G on-chip temperature sensor at a minimum sampling rate of 1 Hz during normal operation. The safety software SHALL implement two temperature thresholds: (1) a WARNING threshold at 120 degC junction temperature — on exceedance, a thermal warning DTC SHALL be set and a thermal warning telltale SHALL be activated; (2) a SHUTDOWN threshold at 125 degC junction temperature (the rated maximum junction temperature for the CYT4B device family) — on exceedance, the safety software SHALL initiate the safe state transition defined in SYS-IC-SAF-012 to prevent operation outside the device's rated thermal envelope. Temperature sensor readings SHALL be cross-validated at startup against the TRAVEO T2G factory-calibrated temperature sensor trim values.
requirements.rationale: Operation above the rated maximum junction temperature degrades CMOS transistor reliability, increases susceptibility to bit-flips, and can accelerate failure of the memory arrays and clock oscillators that underpin other safety mechanisms. ASIL-A is assigned because thermal shutdown is a secondary protective measure; the primary thermal management responsibility belongs to the system thermal design. The 120 degC warning threshold provides a 5 degC margin before the SHUTDOWN threshold, allowing controlled diagnostic action before hard shutdown. Reference: Infineon TRAVEO T2G Datasheet, Table — Thermal Characteristics; TRAVEO T2G Safety Manual, Section 12 (Temperature Sensor).

requirements.title: Periodic runtime self-test (LBIST and periodic BIST routines)
requirements.text: The instrument cluster safety software SHALL execute periodic self-test routines during vehicle operation to detect latent faults in hardware elements not covered by continuous monitoring mechanisms. The periodic self-test schedule SHALL include: (1) a periodic CPU register and ALU self-test using the TRAVEO T2G CPU Test Controller routines, executed at a minimum interval of 100 ms and within a dedicated time slot that does not violate the application real-time schedule; (2) a periodic SRAM single-cell test on a rotating subset of SRAM banks, completing a full-bank cycle within 1000 ms. Each periodic self-test SHALL complete within the Fault Tolerant Time Interval (FTTI) derived from HARA HA-IC-SAF-002 to ensure latent faults are detected before they can combine with a second fault to cause a hazardous event. Any periodic self-test failure SHALL be treated as a non-recoverable fault and SHALL trigger the safe state transition defined in SYS-IC-SAF-012.
requirements.rationale: Startup BIST (SYS-IC-SAF-002) detects faults present at power-on but cannot detect faults that develop during vehicle operation. ISO 26262 requires that latent faults are detected within a defined multiple-point fault latent time interval. Periodic runtime self-tests constitute the safety mechanism for latent fault detection during the operating cycle, enabling achievement of the LFM target for the CPU and SRAM subsystems. The 100 ms CPU test interval and 1000 ms full-SRAM cycle are sized against the FTTI of HA-IC-SAF-002. Reference: Infineon TRAVEO T2G Safety Manual, Section 5.3 (Periodic Self-Test Configuration and Scheduling).

requirements.title: Safe state definition and controlled transition
requirements.text: The instrument cluster safety software SHALL define and implement a safe state that is entered upon detection of any non-recoverable fault from the safety mechanisms defined in this specification. The instrument cluster safe state SHALL be: (a) disable all active display output zones except a full-screen red fault indicator with a static warning symbol; (b) assert the cluster fault signal on the diagnostic CAN channel within 50 ms; (c) disable all cluster output drivers (backlights, indicator LEDs, audible chime output); (d) write a safe-state entry record to NVM including the triggering fault source, fault timestamp, and odometer value; (e) cease all CAN message transmission except diagnostic NM and fault notification frames. Transition from normal operation to safe state SHALL complete within 100 ms of fault detection. The cluster SHALL remain in safe state until an explicit external reset (KL30 power cycle or ECUReset diagnostic service). The safe state SHALL be reachable from any system operating state including startup, normal operation, and shutdown.
requirements.rationale: ISO 26262 requires that every safety goal has an associated safe state into which the system can transition upon fault detection. For an instrument cluster, the safe state is defined as the condition in which the cluster can no longer display misleading information — a full-screen fault indication prevents the driver from acting on potentially erroneous data while ensuring the driver is aware of the fault. The 100 ms transition time is derived from the FTTI of HARA HA-IC-SAF-001. NVM logging of the safe state entry record is required for post-event diagnostic traceability per ASPICE SUP.9 and ISO 26262 Part 8 confirmation measures.
